pages.full_text:
When we arrived at Waddanhoe **2
we had tea + set off on the Nene Valley
Walk from the campsite, just a [?second]
of field below a hodge beside the road.
A very muddy trail. We reached the church
on a little hill. An old font. There were
pilgrim walkers. Down the footpath, past
the church hall, we reached the very
pretty ~~village, partly thatched. Very few
cars. The pub is the King's Head. Beer.
We saw a lovely manor house on the hill,
+ a Mill House. We wandered around the
watermeadow + looked at the lock.
    After a drink at the pub, we got
back to the van, walking along the road,
at 7.30. Tonic has been stung by
nettles + is much wiser now. He slept
the night in the van.
    April 5th
    We drove into __Oundle__ + found a
pages.notes: Handwritten page number not clearly visible in top-left corner of this page. Top of left image shows 'When we arrived at Waddanhoe **2' — the '**2' at top right edge of this scan may be a page number on the adjacent right page or an annotation; not assigned as handwritten_page_no for this page. The word after 'just a' is partially legible — possibly 'second' (as in a short stretch) but could be 'sense'. The diarist struck through a word before 'village' (marked ~~); the struck-through word appears to be 'or'. 'Tonic' appears to be a person or pet name. 'Waddanhoe' is the diary's spelling of Wadenhoe (village in Northamptonshire on the Nene). April 5th 1998 was Palm Sunday, which fits with the right page — year is uncertain but 1998 is the most likely year within 1989–2003 for Palm Sunday on April 5th. 'hodge' likely = 'hedge'.

pages.full_text:
procession for __Palm Sunday__ outside the
headmaster's house. We sang hymns
at a few places + reached the church
which was welcoming. Thomas left a
message on the mobile phone to pick up
Matt Moon + him at 1.45.
    After ~~lunch~~ ~~a~~ church we had coffees
in the van + saw a bit of Oundle.
After lunch at the Coffee Tavern we picked up
Thomas + Matt Moon at the school. We have
set off through rainstorms for home. Matt is
leaving at New St. Sta. for Bristd.
    April 7th.
    Peter, John, Charles, Eve left for
__Beauchamp__ at 8.30. am. John is on a
music course. After dropping John we
went to call on Wendy King. They were house
tidying. The children played with the children in
the garden + walked. Eve had 2 cups of coffee.
Then Wendy led us to Robert Hammond's
house at __Taynton__. Apparently Gus King +
pages.notes: Handwritten page number '3' visible at top-right corner. Page continues directly from left page (sentence begins 'procession for Palm Sunday...'). The diarist struck through 'lunch' and 'a' before writing 'church'. 'New St. Sta.' = New Street Station (Birmingham). 'Bristd.' is likely the diary's abbreviation/misspelling of 'Bristol'. 'Beauchamp' here likely refers to Beauchamp College (in Oadby, Leicestershire) where John attended a music course. 'Taynton' is a village in Oxfordshire. 'Palm Sunday' being underlined. The year 1998 is a strong guess because Palm Sunday fell on April 5th in 1998 within the 1989–2003 range; 1992 is another possibility. Text cuts off at bottom with 'Apparently Gus King +' continuing to next spread. 'Bristd.' could also read 'Bristol' — the 'd' ending is unusual.
